'''Luke Dorn''' (born 2 July 1982) is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|fb|so}} and {{rlp|sh}} in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]] and the [[Super League]] in the 2000s and 2010s.

He played for the [[Northern Eagles]], [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]] and the [[Sydney Roosters]] in the [[National Rugby League]]. He played for the [[London Broncos]], [[Salford City Reds]], [[Harlequins RL]] and the [[Castleford Tigers]] in two separate spells in the [[Super League (Europe) Limited|European Super League]].

==Background==
Dorn was born in [[Maitland, New South Wales]], Australia, and played junior rugby league for the East Maitland Griffins and Morpeth Bulls.<ref name="NH12Jul18"/> He represented the [[Australia national schoolboys rugby league team|Australian Schoolboys team]] in 2000 whilst attending [[All Saints College (Maitland, New South Wales)|Maitland All Saints College]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Australian Schoolboys|url=http://www.sportingpulse.com/assoc_page.cgi?c=7-2130-0-0-0&sID=26424|work=Australian Secondary Schools Rugby League|publisher=sportingpulse.com|access-date=13 April 2011|date=January 2011 }}</ref><ref name="NH17Nov16" />
 
==Playing career==
Dorn made his first grade debut for the Northern Eagles in round 4 of the [[2002 NRL season]].  He played in the club's final game in round 26 of the same year when they lost to Penrith 68–28.  The following year, the Northern Eagles reverted to Manly and Dorn played in their first game back in the competition, a 20–30 loss against North Queensland at [[Dairy Farmers Stadium]].<ref name="SMH18Sep03">{{cite web |title=Manly tell Dorn: look elsewhere |url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/manly-tell-dorn-look-elsewhere-20030918-gdherm.html |website=The Sydney Morning Herald |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=18 September 2003}}</ref><ref name="NH17Nov16" /> 
[[File:Luke Dorn.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Dorn in action for Castleford]]
In 2005 he moved to the [[Super League]] competition, joining the [[London Broncos#1994–2005: Broncos and Super League|London Broncos]], scoring 23 tries in 28 games in his first season and developing a reputation as a prolific try scorer.<ref name="Tel20Sep06" /> In 2007 Dorn moved to the [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]],<ref name="MEN15Feb07">{{cite web |title=Salford land Dorn on two year deal |url=https://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/salford-land-dorn-on-two-year-1038578 |website=Manchester Evening News |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=15 February 2007}}</ref> but was less successful and the team was relegated at the end of the season. Dorn then joined the [[Castleford Tigers]] in 2008, scoring 19 tries in 26 games,<ref name="LRL10Sep13">{{cite web |last1=Gordon |first1=James |title=Luke Dorn returns to Castleford |url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/luke-dorn-returns-to-castleford/ |website=Love Rugby League |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=10 September 2013}}</ref> before returning to the [[London Broncos]] (then known as [[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins RL]]) in 2009.<ref name="Tel20Sep06">{{cite web |last1=Burke |first1=David |title=Harlequins coach Brian McDermott gets his man |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/4324157/Harlequins-coach-Brian-McDermott-gets-his-man.html |website=The Telegraph |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=20 September 2006}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.thisislondon.co.uk/standard-sport/article-23640140-dorn-of-a-new-era-at-harlequins.do|archive-url=https://archive.today/20120912052943/http://www.thisislondon.co.uk/standard-sport/article-23640140-dorn-of-a-new-era-at-harlequins.do|url-status=dead|archive-date=12 September 2012|title=Dorn of a new era at Harlequins|date=12 February 2009|work=Evening Standard|location=London|access-date=9 December 2011}}</ref> In total Dorn played for the [[London Broncos]] 160 times in two spells, becoming the club's all-time record try scorer with 104 tries. He scored his 100th try for the Broncos in a spell where he scored eight tries in two back to back games.

At the end of 2013, Dorn signed for the [[Castleford Tigers]] for a second time.<ref name="BBC10Sep13">{{cite web |title=Luke Dorn: Castleford sign London Broncos utility back |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/24031048 |publisher=BBC Sport |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=10 September 2013}}</ref> Castleford coach, [[Daryl Powell]], said of him: "Luke is a player I have admired for a long time, his ability to play at half-back or full-back is massive and his leadership qualities and experience make him a key recruitment for us next season."<ref name="BBC10Sep13" />

He enjoyed a career resurgence in 2014 and appeared in the [[2014 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Wembley Stadium]],<ref name="Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall’s double stuns Castleford">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/aug/23/catleford-leeds-challenge-cup-final-match-report|title=Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall's double stuns Castleford|work=The Guardian|location=London|date=23 August 2014|access-date=12 November 2019}}</ref> his first major final.<ref name="NH17Nov16" />

The following year, he signed a one-year extension to his contract.<ref name="BBC1Apr15">{{cite web |title=Luke Dorn: Castleford Tigers half-back signs contract extension |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/32138035 |publisher=BBC Sport |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=1 April 2015}}</ref> He played only 14 times in the 2015 season due to injuries, scoring ten tries.<ref name="ITV13May16">{{cite web |title=Dorn to hang up boots at end of season |url=https://www.itv.com/news/calendar/2016-05-13/dorn-to-hang-up-boots-at-end-of-season/ |website=ITV News |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=13 May 2016}}</ref> Dorn retired from Super League Rugby at the end of the 2016 season and returned to Australia.<ref name="BBC27Sep16">{{cite web |title=Luke Dorn: Family inspired Australia return for Castleford Tigers favourite |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/37487290 |publisher=BBC Sport |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=27 September 2016}}</ref> He played for [[Maitland Pickers]] in 2017, becoming player-coach for the 2018 season before retiring from playing at the end of the year.<ref name="NH12Jul18">{{cite web |last1=Callinan |first1=Josh |title=Newcastle Rugby League: Maitland Pickers captain-coach Luke Dorn set to finish decorated playing career |url=https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/5522869/dorn-to-retire-at-end-of-season/ |website=Newcastle Herald |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=12 July 2018}}</ref><ref name="NH12Aug17">{{cite web |last1=Hartshorn |first1=Michael |title=Maitland Pickers name Luke Dorn as first grade coach |url=https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/4850511/pickers-name-luke-dorn-coach/ |website=Newcastle Herald |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=12 August 2017}}</ref><ref name="NH17Nov16">{{cite web |last1=Callinan |first1=Josh |title=Pickers recruit Luke Dorn back in Maitland after 12 years in English Super League |url=https://www.newcastleherald.com.au/story/4299432/dorn-arrives-home-for-next-career-adventure/ |website=Newcastle Herald |access-date=3 July 2019 |date=17 November 2016}}</ref>
